then ever since then, I've been super engaged in everything that you guys are doing.

So you can like learn more on and improve the product that way, like just makes so much sense.
So, um, I, that's also one of the things that, that sold me.

Um, so I lead the customer ops org, which just means, uh, I help like organize the delivery team, um, and think about kind of throughput capacity, like, and own essentially like the renewal and ongoing engagement, um, uh, uh, uh, uh, uh, uh, uh
Sort of KPIs.
So the managing director role, I think it's like we have gone in a lot of directions in terms of what the delivery org looks like.
A lot of agencies have a pod model, and that's what we use as well.

And the managing director, the vision is for that person to own, I think, like 15 accounts as we grow, and then have a strategist, an account strategy, three account strategists with managing editors underneath them in that pod model.
So capacity will depend on kind of how quickly the product can scale up.
So it looks a little bit different than that today, but as we're hiring, we're sort of moving towards that model and building new teams that look more like that structure.
And we have hired, like, I think what took us a little while in the managing director role was hiring people who had been internal as, like, done SEO content strategy, that sort of thing internally.
But one of the things that makes a managing director successful, we're finding, is that ability to kind of do both, right?
Look outward, talk to clients, and be that, like, account relationship owner, and then work internally on, like, the things that define growthx, right?
So that, like, strategy, and then production at scale.
And so you're the team of strategists and managing editors are in the product day-to-day generating that content.
But that ability to kind of get in the weeds, understand how the workflows are put together, and help kind of improve the product and the process of...
Like that input is super important.
I think our managing editors, we have a super strong layer there of team that's like really editorially strong.
They have like that either like journalism or like agency background of just that rigor of editorial quality, which is amazing.
But I think the managing director can help take a step back, be more strategic, and also think about efficiency of like the team that they're owning as well.
Any questions on that, on what that looks like?
No, it all makes sense.
Okay, cool.

I'm sure.

Some people throw that around, but like, truly I'm made for this role, like the, so to explain a little bit why I've worked kind of as a director or senior director position now for a number of years within content SEO orgs, but I've also spanned over into product marketing and pretty much everything under the sun that has content on it.
Um, built systems, hire teams, grew teams, you know, really was very thoughtful always about like, how is the team feeling morale, keeping like the momentum going, all those kinds of things.

Built, worked, and grew teams, very much comfortable with that.
Having managed multiple clients, comfortable with that.

What's the seasonality look like?

Or during my conversation with Marcel, of seeing the back end of how you guys are doing things.

So anyway, all to say, I really like the focus on hiring smart editorial teams, and then leveraging technology the way that it's supposed to be leveraged.
Awesome.
Perfect.

Sensibility.

So talk to me about like owning multiple accounts and or multiple deadlines and how you kind of like think about prioritization and project management across either different accounts or different initiatives that you're owning.

Like how do I balance the two or how am I, how am I managing a prioritization in one and then How do you manage multiple, like really it's thinking about multiple accounts and the ability to structure your time in a week, managing people and accounts.
Like you own a team that's responsible for executing.
I mean, we're putting out like five pieces of content per client per week.
So, you know, it's an upwards of that, like for some clients where we're doing refreshes, we're doing like 50 pieces a week.
So thinking about like structuring the team for delivery.
And making sure that, like, you're paying, spending your time in the right ways for your clients.
And if there are tools that you use, like, feel free to include that.
Sure.

So organizational structures, creating SOPs, creating systems is the first place I always start with, broadly speaking.
Before Aeroops and before all those things, I was pretty early on of, like, seeing ChatGBT as just, like, absolute game changer.
So it was very, very focused on creating kind of repeatable prompts and structures for my freelance team.
So anyway, that's just one small example.
But broadly speaking, I'll use the example of AeroDNA and Uplisting as a good example.
So I have one person focused on each one.
And the core element is, number one, is creating very, like, absolute clarity on what your goals are.
Making sure that you are able to check in on a pretty regular basis.
I actually, I do daily huddles.
toちは, primary Thank
This is something I picked up a little while back when I thought I wanted to be a scrum master, so I got deep into that space.

Anyway, I'll do daily 15-minute huddles to kind of go over like day-to-day activities, but broadly speaking, every two weeks, so we're doing sprints of two weeks, we'll go over like analytics and structures and how are we doing.
So there's a very clear kind of reporting structure that we do every couple of weeks, and then obviously we have a monthly review.
In terms of the kind of the delegation and the structure, the best I can do, as I said, is creating good systems.
So if the system is in place and they're able to create something that's repeating, then I can kind of step back and then focus on what additional systems can I create.
My focus is on eliminating roadblocks.
So if they come to me and they say, hey, there's this issue where the product team wants this or we need to launch this kind of a thing, then I get hyper-focused on, okay, how do I unblock this?
So a lot of my time will be spent on like cross-functional.
Communications, making sure that everything is running smoothly, that that person isn't blocked.
Yeah, so I would say that those are kind of core elements.
The last thing I would say is it's a lot about experimentation, because especially now we're in a, you know, the ground is shaking beneath our feet.
Traditional SEO is really hurting.
I think every company is feeling that.
You're getting these more and more impressions, and you're ranking better, and yet your clicks are staying the same.
So your click-through rate is just, you know, in pain.
And obviously what we're seeing now is this referral traffic coming from LLMs really grow significantly.
But it's still a tiny percentage of your total traffic.
It's like 2%, 1.5%, at least for us it is.
So even though the conversion rate is really good, we're not figuring out how to grow that yet.
It's all new territory.
So with that in mind, I've created a system and a structure for us to be able to quickly experiment with different workflows, with different types of content.
In order to see what works.
And as soon as we identify something doesn't work, an experiment doesn't work, we bin it and we try the next thing.
So we typically have about six different experiments running.
And then we have like maybe three in the background.
And as soon as we see one isn't working, which we give it like four to six weeks to see if it works, then we move on to the next one.
So yeah, delegations, systems, experimentation, dealing with any kind of roadblocks.
Those are kind of the core ways that I work.
Yeah, I mean, that's totally Marcel talks a lot about like, get signals fast, so you can like, know where to focus your time fast.
So that's great to hear that makes a lot of sense.
Can you talk to me, give me an example of a situation where you were responsible for driving a strategic initiative, but you had
Drive the path forward yourself, so I think the question is like establishing clarity and aligning others, like kind of executing an ambiguity, really.
Let me repeat the question just to make sure I understood it.
So what is an example of a time where I had to step in personally to try and make a situation, like a bigger project that was a bit ambiguous, less ambiguous?
Yeah, so, yeah, like the specific term is driving a strategic initiative.
So like, did you have to own, were you responsible for moving a metric or like kicking off a project and owning the results, something like that?
Yeah, sure.
Lots of examples.
I'll use one recent one that we have, where we wanted to, one of the core ways that Erdina has won is with programmatic content.
And it, but it's been very challenging to, to build more programmatic content because we have to work with the product and, and.
The engineering and data teams, and they have their own goals and structures.
So getting them to give a little bit of their time and input our proprietary data into our programmatic content is just really, really challenging.
And so I had one of my team members who was working on this, and it just wasn't quite working.
And I think part of that was maybe because we hadn't told a clear enough and good enough story of, like, this is why this is worthwhile.
So I stepped in personally, I worked directly with the data research and the product team to kind of explain the benefits of this thing.
Then I was just, you know, really instrumental to pulling all of these different threads together.
And the programmatic content that we did was top Airbnbs in market, like in California, in Texas.
So there was 50 of these pages that we produced and, yeah, just got involved, unblocked it as best I could, and then got the data that we needed, the inputs that we needed.
Coordinated all that, and then was able to launch the content.
Awesome.
Okay, and then I only have one question left.
So can you talk to me about, like, you touched on this a little bit, but I guess this is more of a direct question about your management experience.
So how do you divide work across your team today?
I mean, it's really hyper-focused on their specialties, right?
So, like, our content marketing specialists, our social media, because I also manage social media.
I also manage affiliate marketing that's under the organic growth.
So I have specific people working in each one.
And so that's really, sorry, this is maybe not the best answer, but that's okay.
Yeah, that's broadly how I delegate it.
Yeah.
Yeah, and then thinking about that experience.
Yeah.
What's been your biggest mistake as a manager?
Oh, that's always a good question.
I actually, I have a really good answer for this because I just had a good like revelation moment last Friday.
I went to one of my content marketing specialists and I had just been to the GrowthX webinar.
And I had been writing a ton of notes and was like thinking a lot about, okay, what can we implement?
What can we do for Uplisting?
Because Uplisting is kind of the bit of the black sheep in the company.
It's hard to get more resources there.
Anyway, I came in real hot into that meeting and I was like, this is what we should do.
I have this idea.
have this idea.
And she was like, I am so overwhelmed with what I'm already doing.
And she's like, I love the excitement that you have, but we can't do more right now.
And I think that is one of my weaknesses is that I...
I get very enthusiastic about a lot of different things, and I try to do too much.
And it does stress my team out a little bit sometimes.

I think it was a good moment of realization of like, oh, okay, I need to pay more attention to the fact that right now there already is a feeling of like the leadership keeps changing directions on what our focus should be.
And they're just feeling a bit stressed out about that.
And so what I need to really focus on is protecting their energy as much as I can and making sure that they can just focus on their day-to-day and not have to constantly be adapting.
And that's the thing is like, that's, I would say one of my biggest strengths is I'm a constant adapter.
And I'm very curious and I'm always absorbing new information.
But like, just unfiltered spitting that back out to my team, to folks that may not have the same passion perhaps as I do.
We won on that kind of stuff.
It can, it can just feel overwhelming to them.
Yeah.
And what was, what has been your biggest accomplishment as a leader?

I think, honestly, my biggest accomplishment is like, is getting really positive feedback from, from folks that I've worked with that have said that it was, you know, some of the best experience that they've had.
I had a freelancer, actually, we had to let her go at the start of the year when we got Aerofs, actually.
reduced our budget by 75% thanks to this tool.
And in essence, we had to, we had to let people go.
And she left me a really beautiful voice message just saying like, yeah, this is one of the best people, like teams I've gotten to work with.
I think, yeah, my, my biggest accomplishment as a manager specifically is I think I have really high EQ.
I pay a lot of attention to not just like, how's your work?
my, Thank
How are you feeling?
I think I check in a lot on my team on these kinds of things.
There was a period of time, for instance, where we were, we had just been burning the candle on both ends for too long.
And I said to my team, I was like, you need to be at a six or seven energy level out of 10 for the next couple of weeks.
And if you're not, then we need to have a discussion about it.
It's okay if like you have one week where you're at a nine or a 10, but we can't be at a nine or 10 consistently always right now because you're just, you're too tired.
And I can tell the work is starting to suffer.
And so at every Friday, we would stop and go, okay, what number were you at the end of the week?
And then discuss what we can do to maybe alleviate that a little bit.
So just understanding that it's, it's a marathon, it's not a sprint.
And it's really important to make sure that people are feeling seen and heard.
Yeah.
Don't know if that's the best answer, but it's really helpful.
I think we think about that.

But a lot right now, especially as I said, like things are changing, we're like changing the definition of roles and thinking a lot about like building leverage and it's a little chicken or egg right now in terms of like, can we stop hiring because the product is getting better?
How do people adjust their work?
You know, and that that's extra energy.
And so I think, like, we are going back and forth and having that discussion, like at a leadership level all the time.
So, but it's like, that's always, how's the team, like, how are people feeling is, I think, super important.
So that's great to hear.
Sorry.
Yeah, I would just add one more thing, because it's very connected to this.
It's good to manage, obviously to manage down well, but it's almost just as important to manage up well.
And I think that's something that, especially the last two years, I've, I've learned a lot because we've gone through two different CEOs with vastly different energy and desires.
went through two different, you know, products, leads.
Heads of product at the company as well, which has a big impact as well, and I feel like I've had to learn a lot about, a lot more about managing that.
Our new CFO also is like super intense.

So all to say, I think I've gotten, especially recently, a lot better at telling a clear, concise story that does speak to the ultimate goals of the company and doesn't get too caught up in the weeds, but is realistic about like, this is where we're at, this is what we need.
And yeah, it can speak to somebody who knows nothing about content and SEO, but really makes a good case for what we're doing.
Yeah, great.
Well, I guess this is, it's a good segue.
How would your manager have rated you as a leader out of 10?
I mean, we did a review, like maybe.
Four months ago or five months ago, and the review was generally very positive.
The one thing that he said, which is the same thing I just said, is like, you try to do too much.
It's something I think I'm always going to have to keep working on and learning, is I just get too enthusiastic about things.
And I know that might sound like a humble brag or whatever, but it's really not.
Having a manager that wants you to do too much is just straight up a tough thing to experience.
So I understand that that's not, that's really not a good thing that I have to work on.
So I guess I would say, like, I think I'm a solid eight.
I think there's room for improvement with, like, managing how ambitious you are with your goals and what you want to try and do.
And making sure that your team feels like what you're asking of them is really realistic.
Yeah.
Um, I'm never putting you in a room.
I'm
Marcel on, just for you two to plan, because he also, too much, so.
But I am, I'm working on it.
I promise you, this is, this is something I'm really working on.
Bridget Meyer, I was like, wait.
Yeah, cool.
Well, those are all my questions.
What questions do you have for me?
Yeah, this is going to be straight out of left field, but it's, I'm asking it because this was in my kind of loom pitch to, to Marcel.
Marcel, I had said to him, because I figured you had a lot of people like me already.
And so I was like, what is different that I could offer?
And I was like, well, what's different is I'm trilingual.
speak French and Spanish.
I have done a lot of, you know, growth in international.
The previous company, too, I worked at was French, it was English and Spanish.
It was kind of focused on the Latino community in the States.
Anyway, all to say, like, I don't know if that's.
In your longer term goals of growing growthx internationally, but I was curious to know if that that was something on the radar.
It's not something I need to do.
I just thought, hey, that's something that I'm particularly capable of.
I know that generally speaking, Europe is always like two to three years behind the States.
And so there's always a big opportunity to come in early if you have a really great solution, which I think growthx does.
So, yeah, I mean, it is an interesting question, especially because our team is so international, like specifically like our strategists and managing editors are mostly outside of the US.
And so it would make sense just for us to be able to like serve some of those orgs, like near to countries where they're living.
We have one client where we have done localization for them that's based in Mexico.
Let's go.
Um, and we do like all of their articles in English and Spanish.
Um, and I think that that sort of offering would be, yeah, really interesting.
Like, certainly would be interesting.
It's, we got to get the product up and running and think and like really hone in on the model that we would want.
But we haven't said no to anybody because of language so far.
So it would be nice to be able to like also be more, I mean, the, some of our clients like are their language offerings for like speech to text and things like that.
Like it would be interesting to think about, um, you know, building content for them in other languages, especially if they're thinking about like voice recognition and translations.
We have a couple of really big clients that do that.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
So that's a yes, we'd be open to it.
It hasn't sort of been a priority yet.
Understand.
Right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
If it came.

It would be nice to be able to say we could help you, but pretty much all of our, everything that we've had so far, like all of our business is inbound, like we're, like Tyler's fending off, our account executive is fending off leads and telling them, let's talk in January.
So I guess we haven't thought about like where else we need to go to get business or should go.
Yeah, yeah, just, yeah, makes sense.
If you have explosive growth in the States, then just handle that for now.
Yeah, cool.
Okay.
Another question I had was like reading through the job description, it mentions a number of times, like you need to be the type of person who can manage the team, delegate well, but also step in.
And you kind of touched on that as well.
Broadly speaking, how do you balance that with your team members?
Like, is there an internal system for when is the right time to step in versus not?
Is it just like how?
How is this client?
How important is it?
Or I'm just curious if you guys already have like a system where is each managing director kind of creating their own version of that?
Yeah, right now it's the latter, where depending on the managing director, they're like focusing their time a little bit differently on a given week.
But we are standardizing like that expectation and starting to share that out.
I think the idea in that specifically that sentence is, you know, you trust your team to get things done.
But to something that you said earlier about experimentation, looking for signals and knowing when to change.

I think like, if you trust your team just to keep executing every week without that like ability to take a step back and have a more objective and just like strategic viewpoint, which I think is like, that's what sets, even though we call them account strategies.
Like, that's what sets the directors apart, is the ability to step back and say, okay, it's time to change the channel, we're not getting the signals we want.
Because I've certainly seen managing editors or account strategists, when like, a director doesn't spend enough time on an account, like, the performance isn't what the client wants.
And they're like, well, let's just keep chugging away.
Like, this is the way we've been doing it.
We know how to, like, execute against this keyword analysis, like, da-da-da-da-da.
And there's not, like, that creative thinking.
And I think, like, stepping in is, the director's job to step in is especially important when you need to, like, think about, bring in, like, more creative thinking and shift strategy.
But also, we've got, like, a couple weeks ago, we had, like, one of the team members was out on a director's team, and she just, like, literally was at.
Editing content for a week, like, you know, so it's not that that should be your job.
And, like, other directors are, like, you know, we'll pause production.
There are different ways to manage that, too.
But I think, like, in able to have the ability to really coach your team, you should, you know, know how to step in and speak over for them, too.
I think that's the most important thing, honestly, is, like, you first have to do it yourself.
Yeah.
And really, like, go through the process to know, like, where is this inefficient?
Or where is this trip me up?
And then you can, like, help make sure that it's a better SOP and a better system.
Yeah, yeah.
But for the most part, like, I think the team should be executing, like, on a week-to-week or day-to-day basis.
And it's just you're kind of looking at all the signals and figuring out where you need to focus and spend more time and support the team.
Okay, cool.
I have a broader question.
What do you love the most about working at GrowthX?
What do you find challenging?
I also, like, I'm a big believer in, like, Marcel and also in Daniel.
I just think, like, our founders are so, as much as Marcel, like, wants to change a channel or go after something new, when he brings those ideas, it's like, you're so smart.
How did you, like, where did you come up with this?
Like, the creativity is endless, and the, like, that the opportunities are endless, I feel, as a result.
And I think that that's, like, the energy that brings, that, like, keeps GrowthX moving is, like, Marcel's, like, vision.
And that's not to say that he's not, like, yeah, like, he's not a crazy founder or anything.
He just gets really excited.
No, it's really good to hear, because I've worked in a number of startups.
And...
And...
The crazy founder thing is like, that's very real.

Yeah, well, and Daniel, who's the other co-founder and the head of engineering, like, he has seen this movie before, and is really like, no, we have, you know, firing clients is a big conversation right now.
Like, who's outside of our ICP?
Like, are they just trying to negotiate down for less money?
It's not worth it keeping them.
Are they trying to get like all this custom engineering work for free?
Like, fine, if they threaten to cancel, because that's not work we're gonna do, you know, just like that.
Our ability to say no is, like, really important for our own scalability right now.
And I think, like, Marcel is good at recognizing when he's like, gone too far and coming back and being like, okay, this is fine.
Like, you know, like, let's think about the day to day and prioritize accordingly.
Um, but I think he is, he is really.
But like creative and like can just see like the opportunity and it makes it really exciting.
Okay.
And challenging?
And challenging.

Yeah, I would say that like the most challenging part of my job right now is like capacity planning of we're onboarding two clients a week and we're trying to think, as I said, trying to think about leverage and scale.

Um, and like, I'm constantly trying to find that balance between like, we're onboarding two clients a week, we can't bring on like two new people a week, you know, but we're bringing on a ton of new people.
Um, but you know, what's the realistic, um, balance of what people can do, where the product is today.
We don't want to overhire if like, how fast is the product getting better.
And just like that is, it's kind of a juggling act that.
On a week-to-week basis changes, depending on like several factors.
But I think the challenging part is just making sure that we have the right people in the right place without getting everybody to a nine all the time.
Which people do end up at nines, but hopefully not forever.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
That's good.
If I were to get the job, would you be my boss?
Yes.
I manage the directors.
No, sorry.
I manage the directors.
Okay, super.
Well, I was going to ask, like, what is your management style like?
Just so I can get a sneak peek.
Yeah.
So I, like, my meetings, we have, so I lead the strategy team to, or the execution implementation team.
We call it the strategy sprint as well.
So, like, kind of depends on the team and the level that I'm working with at a given time.
Because I manage the directors.
And so, in a sense, I'm also...
Indirectively managing like the strategists and managing editors.
And so I work a lot with those teams as well.
And I have daily standups with the implementation team doing strategy sprints.
I have a lot of trust in my team.
So I liked a lot of hearing a lot about that.
And the way that I run the director's meetings is like really a discussion of like, let's talk about one instance, one problem.
And I think my priority, especially where we are in the startup today is transparency across like pods and accounts so that people aren't in silos.
So thinking a lot about you're solving this problem, you're solving that problem.
How are we documenting it?
How are we making sure that we're aligning like the engineering ask so that we can streamline kind of the way.

That we're solving problems, but it's, for the directors, I mean, like, I'm sure you're, you're very aware that you're quite senior in terms of, like, experience managing a team, and like, a lot of our other directors are as well, so it's, it's more laissez-faire on, like, the, how you're running your pods.

Um, it's more setting up, like, the structure and visibility, so that when a problem comes up, we either have seen it before, and know who to go to, or, um, I can, like, step in, and I'm often the punching bag when something's going wrong.
I feel like that's my most useful, uh, that's where I'm most useful, is in the crisis, figuring out how we, uh, get somebody resources, and make sure that they're not overwhelmed.
Um, yeah, I meet with all the directors once a week, and we just kind of chat about priorities, and things like that.
That sounds great.
I, I really appreciate the focus on transparency, because, especially in,

Streams that we're quite strong at, right, like standard editorial content, and then PSEO, we like, we have a playbook, it's people, some people are more and less skilled at executing that playbook, just because of like the sort of structured nature of it, that's a little bit more product than, than editorial.
And so that that's just a different muscle.

But I think like, having a director that can think creatively, or they can help define what the additional work streams are that we offer, because we do do custom work for some of our clients, and developing those channels for, you know, to be able to replicate how we set up that one, like, you know, newsletters is an emergency.
work stream for us, right, we're doing a couple, but they all look a little bit different, partially because like the client has requested that, but how do we
Develop the newsletter and things like that.
And I think like, a director that's going to be successful in a year has helped build out like the business's offerings.
Because one side of what we're going to be doing is we're going to have like, qualify clients into this kind of support.
And so some of our clients will just be like pure product, like literally.

literal SaaS clients where like, the tools that are going to be available in Atlas are going to help them like, generate assignments and do like, run kind of the AI visibility work and things like that.
But the other side of it and where our existing model can really grow is, how do we turn this services model into like, a have to
True behemoth of like strength across different strategies, rather than like the two things that we do really well today.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I mean, that sounds really exciting.
So that's cool.
Yeah, I mean, like, I think it is the some of our biggest clients, we're doing some super cool stuff with them.
And it's just like, how do we bring this outside of like these clients, and learn how to support them?
Like, is it, you know, in a more standardized way that we can, like, Marcel's always showing people what we've done for clients like augment code.
But it's like, they're so custom.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
How we make this not custom?
Yeah, yeah.
So building that out, like, and helping the other directors, like, learn how to implement that.
There is a bit of a, I think, they're going to be responsible for renewals and upsells eventually, like right now, it's just like having renewal conversations.

There's not like a number that anybody's beholden to, but thinking about how we grow the business with like our existing client base and grow our offering, because that's like a VC question too, right?
Like that year over year, NRR, you know, all those things.
Yeah, yeah, totally.

Something that we've been playing around with recently that I think could be exactly what you're describing, like something that you productize, is very clearly what's going to work in the future more and more is expert-led content.
So content that has the voice of one of the experts in your company.
But it's very hard to get that expert to give you that information.
So something we've played around with now, and I've now done it with two of our executives, is using Eleven Labs, you can create little AI voicing, you can prompt it things.
And then in the mornings on their walks, they'll just talk to it about a subject.
I automatically get the transcript, put that transcript into Aerox.
And then create the expert-led content in that way.
So that's maybe something you guys could play around with with your clients is like an interpretation with that.
Yeah.
Well, we use, oh, God, who's the 11 Labs?
Deepgram.
So the 11 Labs competitor, direct competitor is one of our clients.
So we would have, and Marcel's experience.
So we would have to do Deepgram for speech-to-text.
Sure.
But we do have like a thought leadership pipeline.
Oh, nice.
Okay.
like YouTube videos, podcasts, things like that.
And either turn them into articles or turn them into like thought leadership.
Or I think we turn them into articles and then we translate it to thought leadership content.
But yeah.
Well, I'm impressed that I share something.
You're like, we did it.
It's done.
But we do.
It still takes so much refinement.
Because as you said, an individual person's voice is like getting that tone right and like generating that thought leadership content.
Content day in, day out.
It's, it's a huge lift.
So yeah.
How do you make it less of huge lift?
Exactly.
So it's something we've done, but how do you turn that into a work stream that we can say, no problem.
Let's add it to your build.
Well, that's when I'd be really interested in, in continuing to refine because yeah.
Anyway, that's, that's great.
Everybody wants it.
Everybody asks about it.
It makes so much sense.
And it's so good for SEO.
Like that's the stuff that really does well.
So totally.
It makes a lot of sense.
Cool.
Well, William, it was great to meet you.
Yeah.
I'm sure you'll hear back soon, but you're onto the next step.
So I'll look forward to chatting with you again.
Great.
Thanks so much.
Thanks.
Bye-bye.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Managing Director role at GrowthX with candidate William Leborgne](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=133.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [William has extensive content/SEO leadership experience and AI workflow expertise, aligning well with GrowthX's needs](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=591.0)
  - [GrowthX is rapidly scaling (2 new clients/week) and seeking directors who can balance strategic thinking with hands-on execution](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2293.0)
  - [Role involves managing \~15 accounts, leading pod of strategists/editors, and helping shape GrowthX's expanding service offerings](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=422.0)
  - [Cultural fit seems strong - William's enthusiasm for AI/experimentation matches GrowthX's innovative, fast-paced environment](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=738.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background

  - [Currently dual role at AeroDNA (data for short-term rentals) and Uplisting (property management tool)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=689.0)
  - [Significant content/SEO leadership experience (e.g. grew AeroDNA blog 328%)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
  - [Launched own boutique agency in 2023 (clients included Credit Karma, TikTok)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=633.0)
  - [Certified Aero content engineer; built 20+ AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=667.0)
  - [Trilingual (English, French, Spanish) with international growth experience](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=1762.0)

### Managing Director Role Overview

  - [Own \~15 accounts, lead pod of strategists and managing editors](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=422.0)
  - [Balance strategic thinking with ability to execute hands-on when needed](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [Help shape GrowthX's expanding service offerings beyond core content/SEO](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2605.0)
  - [Manage client relationships while improving internal processes/product](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [Focus on efficiency, team management, and scaling production (5-50 pieces/week/client)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=847.0)

### GrowthX Company Culture & Growth

  - [Founders (Marcel, Daniel) balance visionary thinking with pragmatic execution](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2158.0)
  - [Rapid growth - onboarding 2 new clients/week, hiring quickly but thoughtfully](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2293.0)
  - [Evolving from agency model to SaaS product (Atlas) with tiered service offerings](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2655.0)
  - [International expansion potential, though not immediate focus](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=1762.0)
  - [Emphasis on experimentation, getting quick signals, pivoting strategies as needed](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=1029.0)

### Management Approach

  - [William's style: Create systems/SOPs, daily huddles, bi-weekly sprints, focus on unblocking team](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=911.0)
  - [Andi (interviewer) emphasizes transparency across pods, laissez-faire with experienced directors](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2373.0)
  - [GrowthX aiming to standardize processes while maintaining flexibility for custom client work](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=1952.0)

### Challenges & Opportunities

  - [Capacity planning amid rapid growth - balancing new clients, hiring, and product improvements](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2293.0)
  - [Developing replicable processes for custom offerings (e.g. newsletters, expert-led content)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2605.0)
  - [Improving efficiency of thought leadership content creation (e.g. speech-to-text pipelines)](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2782.0)
  - [Expanding service offerings beyond core content/SEO strengths](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2605.0)

## Next Steps

  - [William to proceed to next interview round](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2912.0)
  - [GrowthX to evaluate William's fit for helping scale operations and develop new service offerings](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=2605.0)
  - [Consider William's international/language skills for potential future expansion](https://fathom.video/share/VbskfUS-upHEenehT3iQfweX5H8tJ5zu?tab=summary&timestamp=1762.0)
